text: Active cool-downs are largely ineffective for enhancing same-day sports performance compared to passive cool-downs.
Active cool-downs may offer some beneficial effects on next-day performance, though evidence is mixed.
Active cool-downs do not appear to prevent injuries.
Performing an active cool-down on a regular basis does not attenuate long-term training adaptations.
Active cool-downs accelerate clearance of lactate from the blood but not necessarily from muscle tissue.
Active cool-downs do not significantly reduce delayed-onset muscle soreness or improve recovery of indirect markers of muscle damage.
Active cool-downs can interfere with muscle glycogen resynthesis after exercise.
Active cool-downs may partially prevent immune system depression and promote faster recovery of the cardiovascular and respiratory systems after exercise.
